title: Standardization (Z-Score Scaling)
concept: Standardization centers data around a mean of 0 with a standard deviation of 1, making it resilient to outliers.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: If your dataset has a minimum age of 10 and a maximum of 90, what does an age of 90 scale to using Min-Max scaling?
exercises.explain: Correct! The maximum value in Min-Max scaling always maps to 1.0.

cards.title: Relative performance
cards.body: If you got 85% on a math test and 75% on a chemistry test, which was better? If the math class average was 90% but chemistry average was 60%, your chemistry performance was much higher relative to your peers!

cards.title: Standardization
cards.body: Standardization (or Z-score scaling) scales values so the mean becomes 0 and the standard deviation becomes 1. The formula is: (x - mean) / standard_deviation.

cards.title: Grading on a curve
cards.body: Instead of grading on a scale of 0 to 100, think of rating performance based on how many steps above or below average someone is. A score of 0 means exactly average.

cards.title: Home Prices
cards.body: If average home price is $400k with standard deviation of $100k, a house costing $600k has a Z-score of: (600k - 400k) / 100k = +2.0. It is 2 standard deviations above average.

cards.title: Not bounded
cards.body: Unlike Min-Max scaling, standardization does not bound your values to a strict range. An extreme outlier can scale to +5, -10, or more. This is intentional!

left.text:
Strictly bounds values between 0 and 1.
Outliers squish normal values together.

right.text:
Centers at 0, unlimited bounds.
Outliers don't squish normal values.

exercises.prompt: What is the resulting mean and standard deviation of a feature after standardization?
exercises.explain: Standardization mathematically shifts the data distribution to have a center (mean) of 0 and a spread (standard deviation) of 1.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A standardized value (Z-score) can be negative.
exercises.explain: True! Any data point that is below the mean will have a negative standardized value (Z-score).

exercises.sentence: Standardization scales data by subtracting the mean and dividing by the ___.
exercises.explain: Dividing by the standard deviation scales the spread so that one unit of the new scale equals one standard deviation.

exercises.prompt: A dataset of customer heights has a mean of 170 cm and a standard deviation of 10 cm. What is the Z-score of a customer who is 150 cm tall?
exercises.explain: Using the formula: (150 - 170) / 10 = -20 / 10 = -2.0, meaning two standard deviations below the mean.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to predict credit scores. One feature is annual_income, which has a mean of $50,000 and a standard deviation of $15,000. An outlier customer has an income of $200,000. If you apply standardization, what will this outlier's value become, and what does it represent?

options.text: +10.0, meaning their income is 10 standard deviations above the average
1.0, because it is the maximum value

exercises.explain: Correct! (200k - 50k) / 15k = 150k / 15k = +10.0. This tells the model the customer is 10 standard deviations above the average income. Sensational work conquering Z-score standardization!
